---
title: "COVID-19 in Czechia, in Context"
subtitle: "Comparing countries fairly using per-capita daily case increments"
description: "A walk-through of the full data pipeline — from raw Johns Hopkins case counts to a ranked, population-normalized comparison of countries."
author: "Josef Richter"
date: "2023-03-09"
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
---

## The question

During the pandemic, raw case counts dominated the headlines — but a raw count of
"new cases today" tells you almost nothing on its own. A country with 10 million
people and one with 80 million will report wildly different absolute numbers even
when the outbreak is, proportionally, identical.

This analysis answers a fairer question:

> **On a given day, which countries had the most new COVID-19 cases _relative to
> their population_ — and where did Czechia sit in that ranking?**

To get there we need three things: a reliable daily case series, population data to
normalize by, and a smoothing method to cut through reporting noise (weekends,
batch-uploaded backlogs, etc.).

::: {.callout-note}
The interactive version of this analysis — with sliders for the averaging window,
date, and country selection — is available as a
[**live dashboard**](https://YOUR-APP.streamlit.app). This page shows the full
method and reasoning behind it.
:::

## The data

Two sources:

- **Confirmed cases** — the [Johns Hopkins CSSE](https://github.com/CSSEGISandData/COVID-19)
  global time series, the de-facto standard during the pandemic. One row per
  region, one column per day, cumulative counts. Pulled live over HTTPS.
- **Population** — UN population estimates (`PopTotal`, in thousands), used to
  convert absolute case counts into per-100k rates.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker

plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (11, 5.5), "figure.dpi": 110,
    "axes.spines.top": False, "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True, "grid.alpha": 0.25, "font.size": 11,
})

JHU = ("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/CSSEGISandData/COVID-19/master/"
       "csse_covid_19_data/csse_covid_19_time_series/"
       "time_series_covid19_confirmed_global.csv")

confirmed = pd.read_csv(JHU, index_col="Country/Region")
confirmed.iloc[:5, :6]
```

Several large countries (the US, China, Australia…) are split into provinces or
states across multiple rows. To compare *countries*, we collapse those into one row
each by summing, and drop the geographic helper columns.

```{python}
cases = (confirmed
         .drop(columns=["Province/State", "Lat", "Long"])
         .groupby("Country/Region").sum())
cases.columns = pd.to_datetime(cases.columns)   # turn date strings into real dates
print(f"{cases.shape[0]} countries × {cases.shape[1]} days "
      f"({cases.columns.min():%b %Y} → {cases.columns.max():%b %Y})")
```

## Joining population

The UN file is keyed by country name with population in **thousands**, so we
multiply by 1,000 to get absolute people. We attach it as a new column and drop
micro-states (population < 1,000) whose tiny denominators produce unstable rates.

```{python}
population = (pd.read_csv("populations.csv")
             .groupby("Location")["PopTotal"].sum() * 1_000)

cases.insert(0, "Population", population)
cases = cases[cases["Population"] > 1000]
cases[["Population"]].loc[["Czechia", "Germany", "Sweden"]]
```

## The core metric: average daily increment per 100k

JHU counts are **cumulative**, so the new cases over the last `N` days is simply
*(cumulative today − cumulative N days ago)*. Dividing by `N` gives a smoothed
**average daily increment** — this is what flattens out weekend dips and reporting
spikes. We then normalize per 100,000 inhabitants so countries of any size sit on
the same scale.

```{python}
N = 7  # one week — long enough to smooth reporting noise, short enough to stay current

latest, prior = cases.columns[-1], cases.columns[-1 - N]

cases["NdayIncrement"] = cases[latest] - cases[prior]
cases["DailyAvg"]      = cases["NdayIncrement"] / N
cases["DailyAvgPer100k"] = cases["DailyAvg"] / cases["Population"] * 100_000

ranking = cases.sort_values("DailyAvgPer100k", ascending=False)
ranking[["Population", "DailyAvg", "DailyAvgPer100k"]].head(10).round(1)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## A normalization gotcha
Because `PopTotal` arrives in *thousands*, it's easy to apply the wrong factor and
inflate every rate by 100×. The ranking still looks "right" (order is preserved),
which is exactly why this kind of bug survives a casual glance — the absolute
numbers are what give it away. Here population is converted to absolute people
*once*, up front, so `* 100_000` is unambiguous.
:::

## Where does Czechia stand?

```{python}
#| label: fig-ranking
#| fig-cap: "Average daily new cases per 100k inhabitants, top 25 countries. Czechia highlighted."
top = ranking.head(25)
colors = ["#e4572e" if c == "Czechia" else "#4a6fa5" for c in top.index]

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.bar(top.index, top["DailyAvgPer100k"], color=colors)
ax.set_ylabel("Daily new cases / 100k")
ax.set_xticks(range(len(top)))
ax.set_xticklabels(top.index, rotation=60, ha="right")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

cz_rank = ranking.index.get_loc("Czechia") + 1
print(f"Czechia: rank #{cz_rank} of {len(ranking)} "
      f"· {ranking.loc['Czechia', 'DailyAvgPer100k']:.1f} daily cases / 100k "
      f"· {ranking.loc['Czechia', 'DailyAvg']:,.0f} new cases/day")
```

At this snapshot Czechia sits in the **upper-middle of the global distribution** —
well below the acute outbreaks in Austria and New Zealand, but above most large
European neighbours. The per-capita lens completely reorders the picture you'd get
from absolute counts, where large countries always dominate.

## How did the outbreak evolve?

A single snapshot hides the dynamics. Here is the same per-100k metric plotted as a
**trend over time** for a handful of comparable countries — revealing the timing and
height of each wave rather than a single number.

```{python}
#| label: fig-trend
#| fig-cap: "Daily new cases per 100k (7-day rolling average), last 120 days."
picks = ["Czechia", "Sweden", "Germany", "Italy"]

daily_new = cases.loc[picks, cases.columns[1:-3]].diff(axis=1)  # de-cumulate
per100k   = daily_new.div(population.loc[picks], axis=0) * 100_000
trend     = per100k.T.rolling(N).mean().tail(120)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for country in picks:
    ax.plot(trend.index, trend[country], label=country, linewidth=2)
ax.set_ylabel("Daily new cases / 100k (7-day avg)")
ax.legend(frameon=False)
fig.autofmt_xdate()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The trajectories tell the story the ranking can't: Italy's tall, sustained wave
through late 2022; Germany's slower decline; Czechia's brief sharp spike followed by
a return to one of the lower per-capita rates in the group.

## Takeaways

- **Normalize before you compare.** Per-100k rates and absolute counts produce
  almost opposite country rankings; only the former is meaningful across borders.
- **Smooth, don't snapshot.** A 7-day average increment removes the weekday
  reporting artefacts that make raw daily figures so noisy.
- **Snapshot _and_ trend.** A ranking answers "who's worst right now"; the time
  series answers "and is it getting better or worse" — you need both.

## Reproducibility

Everything above runs from two inputs (the live JHU CSV and a bundled population
file) with no database or API keys. Toggle **"Show code"** on any section to see the
exact transformation. The same pipeline powers the
[interactive dashboard](https://YOUR-APP.streamlit.app).

::: {.callout-warning}
The Johns Hopkins feed stopped updating on **10 March 2023**, so the figures above
reflect the final available data. This is a showcase of the analysis and
engineering, not a live tracker.
:::
